title: SEGMENT 9 — CAR receptor binding: the coxsackie-adenovirus receptor as the primary entry route

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — CAR receptor binding: the coxsackie-adenovirus receptor as the primary entry route
Let's talk about how adenovirus actually finds its way into cells.

The mechanism matters because it determines which cells get transduced when you deliver a vector — the tropism of the virus — and it constrains what kinds of therapies are practical and what kinds aren't.
Most human adenoviral types, including Ad5, bind a cell-surface protein called the coxsackie and adenovirus receptor, or CAR.
The name is a historical accident — the protein was discovered as the entry receptor for coxsackie B virus and for several adenoviral types simultaneously, and the joint name stuck.
CAR is a member of the immunoglobulin superfamily of cell adhesion molecules.
Its natural job in the body has nothing to do with viruses; it's a component of tight junctions between epithelial cells, where it helps neighboring cells adhere to each other.

Adenovirus and coxsackie B virus both evolved to use CAR as a doorway because epithelial cells — the cells lining the airway, the gut, and various ducts — express CAR abundantly on the surfaces facing the lumen.

When an adenoviral particle approaches a cell, the fiber knob at the tip of one of the twelve fibers protrudes from the capsid, and the knob's distal surface — the part farthest from the capsid — has a binding pocket that engages CAR's extracellular domain.
The interaction is tight — submicromolar affinity — and once engaged, the virus is firmly tethered to the cell.
Then a second event happens at the penton base at the corner of the capsid the fiber is anchored on.

The penton base displays a short loop containing an RGD motif — arginine-glycine-aspartate, a classic integrin-binding sequence — and that RGD loop engages integrins on the cell surface, particularly alpha-V-beta-3 and alpha-V-beta-5 integrins.

The integrin engagement triggers the formation of a clathrin-coated pit, the cell internalizes the virus into an endocytic vesicle, the vesicle acidifies, the capsid partially uncoats and escapes into the cytoplasm, the partially uncoated capsid trundles along microtubules to the nuclear pore, and the genome gets injected into the nucleus.
So the entry process has two key receptor engagements: fiber knob to CAR for binding, penton base RGD to integrin for internalization.
Two handshakes, one for the lock-on, one for the pull-in.
Both handshakes have to work for efficient entry.
The tropism implications of this are important.
CAR is highly expressed on differentiated epithelial cells — airway epithelium, intestinal epithelium, hepatocytes facing the bile canaliculi.
CAR is moderately expressed on many other cell types.

But CAR is poorly expressed on a few cell types that you might really want to target, including most cancer cells (which often have down-regulated CAR as part of their loss of epithelial character), hematopoietic stem cells, mature lymphocytes, and many neural cells.

So an unmodified Ad5 vector is great at transducing liver hepatocytes from the bloodstream — too great, actually; the liver acts as a giant sponge for systemic adenovirus — but it's mediocre at transducing tumor cells, blood cells, or neurons.
There's also a quirk that turned out to matter a lot for liver targeting.
When Ad5 is injected into the bloodstream, the hexon protein on the capsid surface binds, with surprisingly high affinity, to coagulation factor X in the blood.
Factor X then bridges the virus to heparan sulfate proteoglycans on the surface of liver hepatocytes, and this factor-X-mediated route dominates over CAR-mediated entry for systemic delivery.
So the liver tropism of Ad5 in vivo is driven less by CAR and more by this hexon-factor-X bridging.

Knowing this has led to engineering strategies — point mutations in the hexon to disrupt factor X binding — that detarget the vector from the liver, which is useful when you want to deliver it elsewhere systemically without losing most of it to hepatic uptake.
The big idea to leave this segment with is that adenoviral tropism is determined by capsid surface interactions — fiber knob, penton base, hexon — that engage specific cellular and serum molecules.
If you want to change the tropism, you have to change those surface interactions.
Which is what the next segment is about: re-targeting by modifying the fiber knob and other capsid features.
The truck has a default delivery route, written into its bumper stickers and hood ornaments.
Change the stickers, change the route.
We are now at the one-hour mark, more or less.
A natural place to pause for ten minutes.

When we come back, we'll get into how researchers re-engineer the surface of the virus to change what cells it targets, the seroprevalence problem that pushed the field toward exotic serotypes, the chimpanzee adenoviral vectors that solved the seroprevalence problem and powered the AstraZeneca COVID vaccine, the full story of the COVID-era adenoviral vaccines, the comparison with AAV that determines which vector you actually pick for a given job, oncolytic adenoviruses that engineer the virus
to attack cancer cells, the use of adenovirus for CRISPR delivery, modern manufacturing, and finally a glimpse of where the field is going next.
Stretch your legs.
We'll resume shortly.
